title: Knight Steps, Rook Strikes
setup: White's rook stands behind the knight on the open **e-file**, with the black king staring straight at both pieces.
hint: The knight is the only thing standing between your rook and the enemy king — move it with check.
explanation:
## The Idea

A discovered attack always has two actors: the **mover** (the piece in front) and the **discoverer** (the piece behind). Here the knight on e4 is the mover and the rook on e1 is the discoverer. As long as the knight sits on the e-file, the rook does no work.

## Why {{Nd6+}} Works

The knight leaves the e-file with tempo. Two things happen at the same instant:

- The rook on e1 now sees all the way to e8 and gives check.
- The knight has landed on **d6**, where it also attacks the king's only running square, **f7**, and stares at e8 a second time.

Black's king is checked by a piece it cannot capture (the rook is far away and defended), and it cannot block on the e-file because there is nothing in between. The only legal reply is to step aside, after which the knight on d6 is excellently placed.

## Why Nothing Else Is As Good

If the knight moves to a square that does **not** also stab at the king's escape (for example a passive retreat), Black simply runs the king to f7 or d7 and the discovered check loses much of its sting. The lesson: when you spring a discovered check, try to land the moving piece on a square that adds to the attack.
